coredata.dc:title: Coffee grounds-derived carbon quantum dots as peroxidase mimetics for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yuyan
Deng, Xiang
Chen, Jiaming
Hu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we reported the eco-responsible synthesis of iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) from waste coffee grounds through a simple hydrothermal method. The Fe-CQDs exhibited high peroxidase-like activity, which could convert 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. After adding ascorbic acid (AA) to above system, the blue solution faded. Based on this phenomenon, a colorimetric method for visual monitoring of H2O2 and AA was developed. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs can be quenched by the formed ox-TMB via inner filter effect (IFE), followed by the recovery upon the addition of AA. Therefore, Fe-CQDs can be acted as a fluorescent probe to detect H2O2 and AA through the “on–off-on” mode. Furthermore, the dual-recognition methods based on Fe-CQDs were used to measure AA content in beverage samples. Thus, this work would shed much light on converting waste into biomass CQDs and their potential applications in biomolecular detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Coffee grounds
Peroxidase-like activity
Dual-recognition
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AA by the colorimetric method
Determination of AA by the fluorometric method
Kinetic analysis and specific activity measurement
Determination of AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful for the support from Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (grant no. 2022CFC034), Y. Zhu acknowledges the financial support from the 14th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2022029).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image; (b) TEM image (insert: size distribution of Fe-CQDs); (c) XRD spectrum of Fe-CQDs; (d) Raman spectrum of Fe-CQDs; (e) FT-IR spectrum of Fe-CQDs; (f) XPS survey spectrum of Fe-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the Fe-CQDs + TMB system by adding H2O2; (b) Calibration curve for H2O2 assay; (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system after adding different concentrations of AA; (d) The linear relationship between ΔA and AA concentration; (e) ΔA of different interferents; (f) ROS capturing experiments for TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs systems; (g) EPR spectrum of Fe-CQDs. (n = 5, *p < 0.05 for each group).
(a) UV–vis spectrum of Fe-CQDs; (b) Emission spectra of Fe-CQDs under different excitations (310–390 nm); (c) PL spectrum of Fe-CQDs.

(a) PL spectrum of the Fe-CQDs + TMB system by adding H2O2; (b) Calibration curve for H2O2 assay; (c) PL spectrum of TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system after adding different concentrations of AA; (d) The linear relationship between (F – F0)/F0 and AA concentration; (e) (F – F0)/F0 of different interfering materials. (n = 5, *p < 0.05 for each group).
Comparison with reported methods for determination AA.

head.ce:title: Coffee grounds-derived carbon quantum dots as peroxidase mimetics for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology
Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry
Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we reported the eco-responsible synthesis of iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) from waste coffee grounds through a simple hydrothermal method. The Fe-CQDs exhibited high peroxidase-like activity, which could convert 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. After adding ascorbic acid (AA) to above system, the blue solution faded. Based on this phenomenon, a colorimetric method for visual monitoring of H2O2 and AA was developed. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs can be quenched by the formed ox-TMB via inner filter effect (IFE), followed by the recovery upon the addition of AA. Therefore, Fe-CQDs can be acted as a fluorescent probe to detect H2O2 and AA through the “on–off-on” mode. Furthermore, the dual-recognition methods based on Fe-CQDs were used to measure AA content in beverage samples. Thus, this work would shed much light on converting waste into biomass CQDs and their potential applications in biomolecular detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the small carbon-based nanomaterials with particle size <10 nm,(He, Xu, Zhang, & Long, 2022) have attracted widespread attention because of their good biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence (PL), surface modification flexibility, and low cytotoxicity (Guan et al., 2023; Kainth, Maity, Shetti, Basu, & Kakarla, 2023). Due to the above excellent performance, they were widely used in biomedical imaging, (Ding, Ji, Wei, Gao, Zhou, & Xiong, 2017) biomedical sensing, (Meng et al., 2022) tumor therapy,(Wu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2023) and catalysis. (Mishra, Basu, Shetti, Reddy, & Aminabhavi, 2019; Muhammad et al., 2022) Typically, the preparation of CQDs could be divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods. Among them, the hydrothermal method, which belongs to one type of “bottom-up” method, was widely used due to its simple process. To data, many small organic molecules such as citric acid,(Dong et al., 2012) ethylenediamine, (Niu, Li, Zhu, Shan, Fan, & Zhang, 2015) and aniline (Sun, Bruckner, & Lei, 2015) have been used as precursors to prepare CQDs through hydrothermal method. However, the high production cost and potential toxicity of organic precursors severely inhibit their practical application (Ren et al., 2022). Therefore, it’s highly urgent to fabricate CQDs with multifunctional applications through a safe, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly strategy.

Recently, some teams have used broccoli,(Deng et al., 2023) wolfberry,(Gu et al., 2021) orange peel,(Hu et al., 2021) etc., to prepare fluorescent CQDs due to their low cost and wide source. Furthermore, the waste biomass such as leaves,(Chellasamy, Arumugasamy, Govindaraju, & Yun, 2022) food waste (Hu, et al., 2021) or plant residues,(Shi et al., 2019) which is usually considered low value or useless, has recently been used as carbon source for the production of carbon-based nanomaterials.(Wareing, Gentile, & Phan, 2021) Coffee grounds, which are one of the solid wastes that associated with coffee consumption, are thrown in the trash with large quantities every year. In general, the waste coffee grounds contain many organic compounds, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, fatty acids, lipids, and other polysaccharides, thus making them one of the best carbon precursors for the preparation CQDs.(Saberian et al., 2021) In addition, the waste coffee grounds are rich in N elements, which can be incorporated into carbon quantum dots through hydrothermal carbonization to yield nitrogen-doped CQDs. Generally, the modified carbon quantum dots exhibited better performance than that of bare carbon quantum dots. (Li et al., 2021) For example, the heteroatom doping can change the internal electronic environment of CQDs, altering their characteristics and extending their applications. The doping CQDs with transition metal ions can increase their structural stability and catalytic activity (Li, Chen, Wen, Peng, Tang, & Qiu, 2022).

Ascorbic acid (AA, vitamin C) is one of the important and efficient antioxidants that could maintain the normal metabolism of the human body. (Wu et al., 2021) Since the human body cannot synthesize AA, it needs to be obtained from daily diets or functional foods. The lack of AA may cause anemia and the excessive AA may cause gastritis.(Hu et al., 2020) Therefore, it is of great significance to establish simple and sensitive methods for AA detection to provide guidance for dietary and clinical disease diagnosis. So far, many methods have been used to detect AA, such as fluorometry,(Luo et al., 2018) colorimetry,(Yadav et al., 2019) electrochemistry,(Malode & Shetti, 2023) and high performance liquid chromatography.(Turak, Guzel, & Dinc, 2017) Among them, fluorometry and colorimetry were of great interest due to their simple process and low equipment requirements. For example, Xu et al. reported N, S-CDs to detect Fe3+ and AA through the fluorescence “on–off-on” model. (Xu, Wang, Sun, Qi, & Zhao, 2021) Li et al. established a colorimetric sensor based on single-atom nano-enzyme (Fe-SAN) for the rapid detection of AA in tropical fruits. (Li et al., 2023) However, most of the reported methods use only single absorption or fluorescence as the detection signal, which was greatly affected by the background signals and environmental fluctuations. Therefore, it is extremely desirable to develop multi-signal sensing mode for AA detection with enhanced reliability and sensitivity.

In this context, herein we synthesized the iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) via a simple and eco-friendly method with coffee grounds as precursor. The fluorescence quantum yield of Fe-CQDs was measured as 4.18% using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as reference. Furthermore, in the presence of H2O2, the Fe-CQDs exhibited high peroxidase-like activity, which could convert colorless TMB to blue ox-TMB. After adding AA to the above mixture, the blue color gradually disappeared and the absorbance decreased at 652 nm. Meanwhile, the formed ox-TMB could quench the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs through IFE, and the addition of AA inhibited the occurrence of IFE, thus restoring the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs. This fluorescence “off–on” model can be used for AA detection with LOD of 0.133 µM. Finally, we applied this smart Fe-CQDs for AA determination in real samples (vitamin C tablets and beverages) through both colorimetric and fluorometric methods with high selectivity and sensitivity. Overall, this work demonstrated the preparation and detection application of coffee grounds-derived carbon quantum dots, which were characterized as green synthesis, environmental friendliness, and high detection selectivity and sensitivity.

Waste coffee grounds were collected from the coffee shop in Wuhan Institute of Technology. Iron chloride hexahydrate, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride hydrate (TMB), and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Macklin (China). Other reagents and chemicals were purchased from J&K (China) and used without further purification. The HR-TEM images were recorded on JEM-2100plus. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was taken on a powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Smart Lab SE). Raman spectra was performed using a Thermo-Fisher DXR spectrometer. A Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two Spectrometer (L1600301) was used to investigate the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+). The absorption spectrum was measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901). A FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (JOBIN YVON Technology) was applied to collect the PL spectrum.

Firstly, the waste coffee grounds were thoroughly dried in the oven. 5 g of waste coffee grounds were then mixed with 250 mg of iron chloride hexahydrate and 50 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring at 70 ℃ for 2 h, the mixture was transformed into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL), followed by heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The resulting solution was then filtrated to remove solid particles, followed by dialysis with 1000 Da for 48 h. Finally, the black powder was obtained by freeze-drying as Fe-CQDs (Fig. 1 ).

5 g of dried waste coffee ground was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring at 70 ℃ for 2 h, the mixture was transformed into a Teflon-lined autoclave followed by heated at 200 ℃ for 4 h. The resulting mixture was filtrated to remove solid precipitation. The obtained solution was then purified by dialysis (1000 Da) for 48 h, and finally freeze-dried to yield CQDs as brown powder.

5 g of dried waste coffee ground was mixed with cobalt chloride hexahydrate (250 mg) and ultrapure water (50 mL). After heated at 70 ℃ for 2 h, the above mixture was transformed to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After heated at 200 °C for 4 h, the resulting mixture was filtrated to remove solid precipitation. The obtained solution was then dialyzed with 1000 Da for 48 h, followed by freeze-drying to yield black powder as Co-CQDs.

The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs was determined by adding TMB as a chromogenic substrate in the presence of H2O2. Specifically, TMB (0.16 mM), H2O2 (0.33 mM), and Fe-CQDs (35 µg/mL) were added to CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (3 mL, pH 4.2). After incubation for 5 min, the absorption spectra were monitored. Meanwhile, the peroxidase-mimicking performance of the control samples (CQDs and Co-CQDs) was evaluated using the same method.

The kinetic parameters of Fe-CQDs were calculated through the Michaelis-Menten model (Guo, Li, Liu, Luo, & Wu, 2021). Specifically, to the CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer solution (pH 4.2) containing Fe-CQDs (20 µg/mL), different concentration of H2O2 and TMB was added. The absorbance of solution at 652 nm (A652) was then measured every 30 s. The Michaelis-Menten constant (
k m
) was calculated according to the follow equation.
1
v 0
=
k m
v m
s
+
1
v m
where
v 0
and
v m
represent the initial and the maximum rates, respectively.
k m
and
s
represent the Michaelis-Menten constant and substrate (H2O2 or TMB) concentration, respectively.

The peroxidase-like specific activity of Fe-CQDs was determined according to the methods reported in the literature. (S. Ding, Barr, Lyu, Zhang, Wang, Tieu, et al., 2023; Jiang, Duan, Gao, Zhou, Fan, Tang, et al., 2018) The specific activity (SA) of Fe-CQDs was calculated using the follow equation:
SA =
V × ( Δ A / Δ t )
ε × l
/
[ m ]
where SA is the specific activity (U/mg); V is the volume of reaction solution (µL); ε is the molar absorption coefficient of TMB substrate (39,000 M−1 cm−1 at 652 nm); l is the path length of light traveling in the cuvette (cm); ΔA/Δt is the initial rate of change in absorbance at 652 nm (min−1); and
m
is the Fe-CQDs weight (mg) of each assay.

To a 3 mL cuvette, CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH 4.2), Fe-CQDs (20 µg/mL), TMB (0.05 mM), and different concentration (2–40 µM) of H2O2 were added. After incubation for 5 min, the absorption spectra of the mixture were measured, and the concentration of H2O2 was analyzed via the A652.

To a 3 mL cuvette, CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (pH 4.2), Fe-CQDs (35 µg/mL), TMB (0.05 mM), and different concentration (2–40 µM) of H2O2 were added. After incubation for 5 min, the PL spectra of the mixture were measured, and the concentration of H2O2 was analyzed via the PL intensity (Ex = 365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA by the colorimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: TMB (0.16 mM), H2O2 (0.33 mM), and Fe-CQDs (20 µg/mL) were soaked in CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (2.74 mL, pH 4.2) for 15 min. The absorption spectrum (550–750 nm) of the above mixture was then monitored. Subsequently, different concentration of AA (3.3–32.3 µM) was added, and the absorption spectrum (550–750 nm) of the above solution were re-measured. The absorbance variation at 652 nm (ΔA = A0 – A, where A and A0 indicate the absorbance in the presence and absence of AA, respectively) was recorded, and the LOD of AA was measured.

For comparison, 19 different interfering materials (GSH, l-Cys, NO2–, S2-, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, glucose, maltose, sucrose, lactose, His, Gly, Arg, Glu, Ala, CA) were selected in interference experiment for AA detection. The concentration of AA, GSH and l-Cys that added was 32.2, 96.6, and 96.6 µM, respectively. The concentration of other interfering materials was 322 µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA by the fluorometric method
ce:section.ce:para: TMB (0.16 mM), H2O2 (0.33 mM), and Fe-CQDs (35 µg/mL) were soaked in CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (2.7 mL, pH 4.2) for 5 min. The PL spectrum (Ex = 365 nm) of the above solution was then measured. Subsequently, various concentration of AA (3.3–32.3 µM) was added, and the PL spectra of the resultant mixture were re-measured. The fluorescence restoring factor (F – F0)/F0 (F0 and F indicate the PL intensity of the Fe-CQDs in the absence and presence of AA, respectively) was recorded, and the LOD was measured.

For comparison, 19 different interfering materials (GSH, l-Cys, NO2–, S2-, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, glucose, maltose, sucrose, lactose, His, Gly, Arg, Glu, Ala, CA) were selected in interference experiment by comparing their PL intensity (Ex = 365 nm). The concentration of AA and other materials was 32.2 and 322 µM, respectively.

At least three parallel groups were set for each experimental group, and data were plotted as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was determined using t-test, and *p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The Fe-CQDs was synthesized from the waste coffee grounds and iron chloride hexahydrate by hydrothermal method (Fig. S1). The TEM images indicated the spherical morphology of Fe-CQDs with an average size of 1.68 nm (Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b, and Fig. S2). The peak at 21° appeared in the XRD diagram of Fe-CQDs (Fig. 2c), indicating the graphitic structure.(Xu, Wang, Sun, Qi, & Zhao, 2021) The Raman spectrum of Fe-CQDs (Fig. 2d) possessed two peaks at 1354 cm−1 (D band) and 1568 cm−1 (G band), ascribed to the disordered surface regions and sp2 carbon networks. In addition, the ID/IG ratio was about 0.93, suggesting a high graphitization degree in Fe-CQDs.(Jeong, Park, Yi, & Yang, 2023) The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2e) was measured to determine the functional groups of Fe-CQDs. The peak at 3437 cm−1 was assigned to the C-OH/OH group. The peaks at 2931 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, and 1387 cm−1 were attributed to CH, CO/CN, CN stretching vibration, respectively.(Meng, et al., 2022) Moreover, the signal at 1027 cm−1 and 595 cm−1 were assigned to Fe-N and Fe-O stretching vibrations respectively, demonstrating the successful coordination of iron. (Wu, et al., 2023).

The XPS was measured to analyze the chemical composition of Fe-CQDs (Fig. 2f). The peaks at 284, 399, 532 and 712 eV were assigned to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p, respectively. The element proportions of Fe-CQDs were 67.57% (carbon), 5.12% (nitrogen), 26.49% (oxygen), and 0.82% (iron), respectively. The presence of the CC/CC (284.4 eV), CO/CN (285.6 eV), and CO (288 eV) bonds were observed in C 1s spectra (Fig. S3 a). The N 1s spectra of Fe-CQDs (Fig. S3b) showed the binding energies at 399.38 and 400.58 eV, ascribed to pyridine N and pyrrolic N, respectively.(H. Ding, Ji, Wei, Gao, Zhou, & Xiong, 2017) The O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. S3c) showed two peaks at 531.58 and 532.68 eV, attributed to CO and CO groups, respectively. Besides, the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. S3d) was fitted into four peaks at 709.78, 713.78, 722.98 and 726.88 eV, attributed to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2 and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively (Song, Ma, Zhu, Chen, Wang, & Lu, 2018). These results suggested the existence of Fe, N and O-containing functional groups on the surface of Fe-CQDs.

To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs, the TMB was applied as chromogenic substrate. The results in Fig. S4a showed that TMB was oxidized to ox-TMB in the presence of Fe-CQDs and H2O2. Meanwhile, different control experiments such as TMB, TMB + Fe-CQDs, TMB + H2O2, TMB + H2O2 + Co-CQDs, and TMB + H2O2 + CQDs were designed to confirm the main factors that affected the catalytic reaction. As indicated in Fig. S4b, the UV–vis absorption signal of TMB or TMB + Fe-CQDs system did not display any obvious peak at 652 nm, suggesting TMB was not oxidized. In contrast, the TMB + Fe-CQDs + H2O2 system visually turned blue, along with the appearance of absorption signal at 365 and 652 nm, indicating the formation of ox-TMB, revealing the high peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs. However, no significant absorption signal was observed in the TMB + H2O2 + Co-CQDs or TMB + H2O2 + CQDs system (Fig. S4c and S4d), indicating the negligible peroxidase-like activity of Co-CQDs and CQDs. Thus, Fe-CQDs exhibited the outstanding peroxidase activity comparing with that of Co-CQDs and CQDs.

The concentration-dependent catalytic activity was examined to investigate the peroxidase activity of Fe-CQDs. As indicated in Fig. S5, the addition of Fe-CQDs considerably increased the absorbance of ox-TMB. Furthermore, the absorption value of ox-TMB enhanced within 20 min, indicating that Fe-CQDs can effectively catalyze H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen. Similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP),(Wang et al., 2018) the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs was affected by pH value and temperature. Therefore, the effects of pH value (2.6–5.8) and temperature (10–60 °C) on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs were discussed. As indicated in Fig. S6a and S6b, the maximum activity of Fe-CQDs was observed at pH 4.2 and 30 °C. Thus, pH 4.2 and 30 °C were selected as the optimal conditions to investigate the catalytic activity of Fe-CQDs. Additionally, Fe-CQDs remained high peroxidase-like activity after preserving at room temperature for one week (Fig. S6c), demonstrating their high stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis and specific activity measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The kinetic parameters of Fe-CQDs were calculated to further investigate the catalytic process. As shown in Fig. S7a and S7c, the Michaelis-Menten curves were obtained at different concentration of TMB and H2O2. Furthermore, the maximal velocity (
v m
) and Michaelis-Menten constant (
k m
) were determined by the Lineweaver-Burk graphs (Fig. S7b and S7d). Among them,
k m
value implied the affinity between the substrate and mimic enzyme, and the smaller
k m
indicated the stronger affinity. For Fe-CQDs toward TMB, the
k m
and
v m
were measured to be 0.255 mM and 4.14 × 10-8 M s−1 respectively, which were much lower than that of HRP and other enzyme mimics. Besides, the
k m
and
v m
were valued as 0.329 mM and 5.72 × 10-8 M s−1 with H2O2 as substrate, which were much lower than that of HRP. Thus, Fe-CQDs possessed high affinity toward TMB and H2O2 comparing with that of most reported mimic enzymes (Table S1). To better quantify the peroxidase-like activity, the specific activity value of Fe-CQDs was calculated according to the reported methods. (Jiang, et al., 2018) The specific activity value of Fe-CQDs was calculated as 0.9012 U/mg (Fig. S8), which was comparable to other reported literature (Dong et al., 2022).

The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CQDs was probably ascribed to its ability to catalyze H2O2 to reactive oxygen species (ROS), thereby oxidizing the colorless TMB to blue ox-TMB. Therefore, the ROS scavengers such as p-benzoquinone (O2 •- scavengers), isopropanol (·OH scavengers), and d-histidine (1O2 scavengers) were used to determine the catalytic mechanism of Fe-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3f, the A652 of TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system was not affected by adding different concentrations of isopropanol or d-histidine. In contrast, the A652 of TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system was evidently decreased in the presence of P-benzoquinone (0.03–0.3 mM), suggesting the O2 •- was the main ROS that oxidized TMB. Furthermore, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiment was carried out to further verify the generated ROS. As indicated in Fig. 3g, the EPR spectrum of the DMPO + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system displayed the typical signal of DMPO/O2 •-, suggesting the generation of O2 •-. (Zhang, Liu, Li, Luo, & Wu, 2023) These results suggested that Fe-CQDs could act as a peroxidase-like enzyme, which could catalyze H2O2 to O2 •-, inducing the oxidation of TMB.

The above experiments verified that Fe-CQDs was able to catalyze the oxidation of TMB to blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. Therefore, the enhanced absorbance of the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system could be used for H2O2 determination by colorimetry. As indicated in Fig. 3a, the absorption of the TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CQDs system displayed the H2O2 concentration-dependent property. The A652 of the ox-TMB was linearly related to the concentration of H2O2 (2–20 µM) (Fig. 3b), and the detection limit (LOD = 3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank samples, k is the slope of the curve) was calculated as 0.476 µM, which was relatively lower than previously reported enzyme mimics (Table S2). Meanwhile, the solution color of the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system gradually turned to blue with the concentration of H2O2, which could be easily distinguished by the naked eyes. When AA was added to the above system, the ox-TMB can be reduced to colorless TMB. Based on the above phenomenon, a colorimetric method for AA determination was established. As indicated in Fig. 3c, with the addition of AA, the A652 gradually decreased along with the blue color fading colorless. As shown in Fig. 3d, the absorbance change (ΔA = A0 – A) displayed the linear correlation with AA concentration, with a linear equation of ΔA = 0.03076 [AA] – 0.05188 (R2 = 0.997). The LOD was valued as 1.56 µM, which was comparable to that of the reported colorimetric methods (Table 1 ). The selectivity of Fe-CQDs for AA assays was examined with GSH, l-Cys, NO2–, S2-, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, glucose, maltose, sucrose, lactose, His, Gly, Arg, Glu, Ala and CA as interfering materials. As indicated in Fig. 3e, the insignificant absorbance change was observed with the addition of most interferents, even though their concentration was 10 times higher than that of AA. The ΔA exhibited a little change with the addition of 3 times higher concentration of GSH and l-Cys than that of AA, probably due to their weak reducibility. Though it was still a challenge to determine AA content in a mixed system of GHS and l-Cys, this colorimetric method still had a certain selectivity for AA determination.

The photophysical properties of Fe-CQDs were verified through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As indicated in Fig. 4 a, the characteristic absorption signal at 292 nm was probably ascribed to the π → π* transition of CC in sp2 domains. The aqueous solution of Fe-CQDs emitted blue emission under 365 nm UV light. Fe-CQDs presented the typical excitation-dependent PL behavior with the maximum emission peak around 440 nm (Fig. 4b and 4c). The fluorescence quantum yield of Fe-CQDs in water was valued as 4.18% using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.55) as reference (Fig. S9). (Li et al., 2021) In order to evaluate the fluorescence stability of Fe-CQDs, the impact of pH value, ionic strength and storage time were studied. As indicated in Fig. S10a, the PL intensity did not display any significant change when the pH vale ranged from 3 to 10, suggesting its remarkable physiological stability. Additionally, there was no noticeable decrease in PL intensity when the concentration of NaCl changed from 0.25 to 1.5 M (Fig. S10b), demonstrating the exceptional stability under high ionic strength conditions. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity did not display any significant variation after storing at ambient condition for 7 days (Fig. S10c), suggesting that Fe-CQDs had the high fluorescence stability.

In addition to the color change of solution, the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs was quenched after adding TMB and H2O2 (Fig. S11a and Fig. S12). This phenomenon did not happen in the Fe-CQDs + TMB or Fe-CQDs + H2O2 system. Furthermore, the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs could be restored by adding AA to the above quenching system (Fig. S11b). To investigate the quenching mechanism, the excitation spectrum of Fe-CQDs and the absorption spectrum of the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system were measured. As shown in Fig. S11c, the absorption spectrum of the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system (λab = 360 nm) was greatly overlapped with the excitation spectrum (λex = 365 nm) of Fe-CQDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CQDs was probably ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE) of the generated ox-TMB. The addition of AA to the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system would reduce the ox-TMB to TMB, thereby inhibiting the occurrence of IFE and restoring the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs (Fig. S13).

Thus, the fluorescence change (on–off-on) of Fe-CQDs provided a feasible way to determine H2O2 and AA through a facile fluorometric method. As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the addition of H2O2 (0–40 µM), the fluorescence of the Fe-CQDs + TMB system gradually quenched. The fluorescence quenching factor (F0 – F)/F0 displayed a good linear relationship (y = 0.01122x + 0.04735, R2 = 0.999) with H2O2 concentration (2–20 µM) (Fig. 5b). Based on this linear equation, the LOD was valued as 0.457 µM, which was comparable to that of the reported fluorometric methods (Table S2). After adding AA to the above quenching system, the PL of Fe-CQDs was gradually restored (Fig. 5c). Based on the above phenomenon, the Fe-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 system could be used to measure the AA content. The linear correlation between PL restoring factors (F – F0)/F0 and AA concentration could be expressed as (F – F0)/F0 = 0.08144 [AA] – 0.31969 (R2 = 0.992) when the AA concentration was ranged from 3.3 to 13.1 µM, and ((F – F0)/F0 = 0.15783 [AA] – 1.25644, R2 = 0.997) with the AA concentration ranging from 13.1 to 32.2 µM (Fig. 5d). Based on these linear equations, the LOD of AA was valued as 0.133 µM, which was lower than that of other reported fluorometric sensors (Table 1). The selectivity of Fe-CQDs for AA assays was examined with GSH, l-Cys, NO2–, S2-, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, glucose, maltose, sucrose, lactose, His, Gly, Arg, Glu, Ala, and CA as interfering materials. As shown in Fig. 5e, for the addition of most interferents, insignificant fluorescence change was observed, even though their concentration was 10 times higher than that of AA. However, GSH and l-Cys had some influence on (F – F0)/F0 of this system, which was only one-half of AA, suggesting the high selectivity for AA determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the vitamin C tablets and three kinds of beverage were selected as practical samples to confirm the practical application of Fe-CQDs for AA detection. VC tablets were obtained from pharmacy and beverage samples were purchased from supermarkets without further purification. To ensure their concentrations were within the determination range, the samples were diluted to the appropriate concentration before assay. As shown in Fig. S14, the tested results through the colorimetric and fluorometric methods were well consistent with the product description of the purchased samples. Besides, the determined recoveries of the samples were ranged from 95.04% to 107.58%, and the RSD of all test results were <8% (Tables S3 and S4), demonstrating the potential application of Fe-CQDs in real sample analysis through optical dual-signal method.

In summary, we fabricated Fe-CQDs via a one-pot hydrothermal method using waste coffee ground as precursor. As a multifunctional mimetic enzyme, Fe-CQDs displayed excellent peroxidase-mimetic activity and fluorescence properties. Benefiting from the absorption and fluorescence double-signal outputs, we have successfully developed the cost-effective and environmentally friendly colorimetric and fluorometric methods for AA assay with low LOD (1.56 µM and 0.133 µM, respectively). Additionally, the high selectivity for AA determination was demonstrated through interference experiment and the practical feasibility for AA determination was proved in beverage samples using the dual-recognition methods. Hence, this work provided an eco-friendly procedure to fabricate carbon quantum dots with high peroxidase-mimetic activity and strong luminescence, which could be applied for AA detection by a dual-recognition assay, demonstrating the great potential in biological and medical assay. In addition, this work may stimulate additional efforts to explore low-cost multifunctional materials and their sensing applications for small biological molecules.

Yuyan Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiang Deng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Jiaming Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhiyuan Hu: Methodology, Data curation. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support from Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (grant no. 2022CFC034), Y. Zhu acknowledges the financial support from the 14th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2022029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136957.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zheng et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MOF-808: A metal-organic framework with intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity at neutral pH for colorimetric biosensing
Inorganic Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, H. Q., Liu, C. Y., Zeng, X. Y., Chen, J., Lu, J., Lin, R. G., Cao, R., Lin, Z. J., & Su, J. W. (2018). MOF-808: A Metal-Organic Framework with Intrinsic Peroxidase-Like Catalytic Activity at Neutral pH for Colorimetric Biosensing. Inorganic Chemistry, 57(15), 9096-9104. http://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acs.inorgchem.8b01097
